title: From Burnout to Breakthrough: Chef Lindsay Smith's Journey of Resilience
guestBio: Lindsay Smith is the owner and executive chef of Nirvana by Chef Lindsay, a beloved farm-to-table restaurant in Laguna Beach that she's operated for 19 years. She started her culinary journey at 15 and became a restaurant owner by 26, building not just a business but a community through her monthly ladies' dinner series and commitment to authentic connection.
excerpt: Chef Lindsay Smith shares her raw journey of running the same restaurant for 19 years while navigating motherhood, divorce, and complete reinvention. This conversation reveals what it really means to lead in your business and your life.
metaDescription: Chef Lindsay Smith shares 19 years of restaurant ownership, navigating divorce, and rebuilding as a single mom entrepreneur.
keyTakeaways: Your business should evolve as you evolve—pivot when life demands it rather than forcing yourself to fit an outdated model.
Relationships are the foundation of long-term business success, often more valuable than formal education or credentials.
Learning to delegate and shift from operator to owner creates space for both growth and personal freedom.
Building authentic community in the digital age requires intentional in-real-life connections and vulnerability.
Major life transitions like divorce can become catalysts for business reinvention and personal empowerment.
Creativity often emerges from necessity and constraint, leading to breakthrough innovations like signature menu items.
Middle age and perimenopause can bring unexpected clarity, confidence, and permission to choose differently.

pullQuotes.text: I realized at my 40th birthday that I couldn't breathe—and I had to make a change
Relationships are more important than education in business
COVID gave permission to choose differently—and that choice can lead to freedom

timestamps.label: Lindsay's early culinary journey starting at 15
The origin story of goat cheese ice cream
The moment at 40 that changed everything
Divorce, buying out her husband, and rebuilding
The birth of the ladies' dinner series
Perimenopause, middle age, and finding clarity

content:
What happens when the life you've built stops fitting who you're becoming? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with Chef Lindsay Smith, who has spent 19 years building one of Orange County's most beloved restaurants while navigating the complex realities of motherhood, divorce, and complete personal reinvention.

Lindsay's story begins with raw determination. At just 15, she entered the culinary world, and by 26, she had manifested her dream of restaurant ownership. But this isn't just another entrepreneur success story—it's a deeply human narrative about what it really takes to sustain a business while your personal world transforms around you.

The Breaking Point That Led to Breakthrough

The turning point came at Lindsay's 40th birthday celebration. "I realized I couldn't breathe," she shares with startling honesty. This moment of clarity wasn't just about personal struggles—it was about recognizing that her business model needed to evolve as she evolved. The woman who had built Nirvana by Chef Lindsay through sheer force of will was ready to step into a different kind of leadership.

This revelation led to one of the most challenging periods of her life: navigating divorce during COVID while simultaneously rebuilding her business as a single mother. But rather than viewing these challenges as obstacles, Lindsay transformed them into opportunities for reinvention. She bought out her husband, rebranded during the pandemic, and shifted from being an operator to truly owning her role as a leader.

From Control to Community

One of the most striking aspects of Lindsay's journey is her evolution from needing to control every detail to creating space for authentic community building. Her monthly ladies' dinner series wasn't born from a business strategy—it emerged from a genuine need for connection that she recognized in herself and other women.

These intimate gatherings have become more than just dining experiences; they're lifelines for women seeking real connection in an increasingly digital world. "Building authentic community requires intentional in-real-life connections," Lindsay explains, and her sold-out dinners prove that women are hungry for this kind of vulnerability and warmth.

The genius of Lindsay's approach lies in understanding that relationships truly are the foundation of long-term business success. While formal education and credentials have their place, she's built her empire on genuine connections, trust, and the kind of service that makes people feel seen and valued.

Innovation Born from Necessity

Some of the most beloved elements of Nirvana, including their famous goat cheese ice cream, were born from necessity and creative constraint rather than elaborate planning. A backyard fig tree became the inspiration for a signature dessert that guests still request years later. This speaks to a broader truth about entrepreneurship: some of our greatest innovations come not from abundance but from making the most of what we have.

Lindsay's commitment to farm-to-table, seed oil-free, non-GMO cuisine wasn't just a trend-following decision—it reflected her deeper values about nourishing her community authentically. This alignment between personal values and business practices has been crucial to her longevity in an industry known for high turnover and burnout.

The Power of Permission

Perhaps the most powerful theme running through Lindsay's story is the concept of giving yourself permission to change course. "COVID gave permission to choose differently," she reflects, acknowledging how the pandemic became a catalyst for many people to reevaluate their lives and businesses.

This permission-giving extends beyond major life changes to daily business operations. Learning to delegate and trust her team allowed Lindsay to step back from the exhausting role of doing everything herself. This shift from operator to owner didn't happen overnight, but it was essential for both her personal well-being and her business's sustainable growth.

Embracing Middle Age and Clarity

In a culture that often fears aging, Lindsay offers a refreshing perspective on middle age and even perimenopause as sources of unexpected clarity and confidence. Rather than seeing these life stages as limitations, she's found them liberating—providing the wisdom to know what matters and the courage to pursue it.

This clarity has informed everything from her business decisions to her parenting approach as a single mother. She's modeling for other women that it's possible to reinvent yourself at any stage of life, that your business can evolve as you do, and that sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is give yourself permission to breathe.

Lindsay Smith's journey reminds us that sustainable success isn't about grinding through at all costs—it's about building something that can adapt and grow as you do. Whether you're running a restaurant, leading a team, or simply trying to manage the beautiful complexity of your own life, her story offers both practical wisdom and profound permission to choose differently when the moment calls for it.

quiz.title: What's Your Reinvention Style?
quiz.description: Like Chef Lindsay Smith, we all face moments when our lives need to evolve. Discover how you naturally navigate change and transformation.

questions.question: When you sense that something major in your life needs to change, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Trust the feeling and start exploring what needs to shift, even if I can't explain it yet
Take time to analyze the situation and create a thoughtful plan before acting
Look for the hidden opportunity in the challenge and get excited about possibilities
Reach out to trusted friends or mentors to process the situation together

questions.question: Your business or career feels stuck in an old version of yourself. How do you approach evolving it?

options.text: Listen to what my authentic self is calling for and honor that vision
Systematically identify what's not working and build new systems gradually
Use the constraints as creative fuel to innovate something completely new
Engage my community to understand what they need and how I can serve better

questions.question: When facing a major life transition like divorce, career change, or empty nest, you tend to:

options.text: See it as a natural evolution and trust the process, even when it's uncomfortable
Focus on building stability in other areas while navigating the transition carefully
Get energized by the blank slate and all the new possibilities ahead
Lean into your support network and be open about what you're experiencing

questions.question: Your relationship with control and delegation is:

options.text: Learning to let go feels scary but necessary for my growth
I delegate systematically, ensuring proper training and clear expectations
I love empowering others to bring their own creative solutions to challenges
I naturally collaborate and prefer shared decision-making over controlling everything

questions.question: When building community or deepening relationships, you're most drawn to:

options.text: Creating authentic connections by being vulnerable about your own journey
Building consistent, reliable touchpoints that people can count on over time
Bringing people together through shared experiences and creative collaboration
Facilitating meaningful conversations and helping others connect with each other

questions.question: Your biggest fear about reinventing yourself is:

options.text: Ignoring my intuition and staying stuck in something that no longer serves me
Making changes too quickly without proper planning and losing what I've built
Playing it safe and missing the opportunity to create something truly innovative
Going through the process alone without the support and input of people I trust

results.title: The Intuitive Transformer
results.description: You have a remarkable ability to sense when something in your life needs to shift before it becomes a crisis. You trust your inner wisdom and aren't afraid to make bold changes when your gut tells you it's time. Your superpower is recognizing patterns and pivoting gracefully, even when others don't understand your decisions yet. Lindsay's story of recognizing she 'couldn't breathe' at 40 and choosing radical change will deeply resonate with your transformative nature.

results.title: The Steady Rebuilder
results.description: You approach change methodically, preferring to build solid foundations before making major moves. While others might rush into transformation, you take time to plan and create sustainable systems for your new chapter. Your strength lies in creating lasting change that stands the test of time. Lindsay's journey of systematically shifting from operator to owner while building authentic community will inspire your thoughtful approach to reinvention.

results.title: The Catalyst Creator
results.description: You thrive on turning challenges into opportunities and often find your greatest innovations emerge from your most difficult moments. You have a unique gift for seeing possibilities where others see problems and inspiring others through your resilience. Your ability to create something beautiful from chaos is truly remarkable. Lindsay's story of transforming divorce and pandemic challenges into business breakthroughs will fuel your creative problem-solving spirit.

results.title: The Connected Collaborator
results.description: You understand that meaningful change happens through relationships and community. You naturally build bridges and create spaces where authentic connections flourish. Your reinvention style involves bringing others along on the journey and finding strength through vulnerability and shared experiences. Lindsay's evolution from controlling every detail to creating community through her ladies' dinners will speak directly to your collaborative heart.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manage Her, the podcast where we turn the invisible work of women into powerful intentional leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manage Her, entrepreneur and mama of six.

Today's guest is someone I've known for years. We actually went to high school together and I couldn't be more excited to share her story with you. Lindsay Smith is the owner and executive chef of Nirvana by Chef Lindsay, a Laguna Beach landmark known for its seed oil-free, non-GMO, farm-to-fork wine country cuisine.

For the past 19 years, Lindsay has built not just a restaurant, but a community. Through seasons of change, rebranding, and reinvention, she's weathered the storms that come with entrepreneurship and motherhood, all while serving up warmth, connection, and creativity to her customers and her city.

Lindsay is also the host of a monthly ladies dinner series at Nirvana, a sold-out gathering of about 50 women that has become a heartbeat of community and conversation in Laguna Beach. As a single mom of two sons, she brings heart, grit, and grace to everything she does, balancing life, family, and business with authenticity and purpose.

Today, we're talking about what it really takes to build something that lasts, the highs and lows of restaurant life, the courage to reimagine your dream, and how food can bring people together. Hi, Lindsay. How are you, honey? I'm so glad you're here today. Finally got you on the show.

**Lindsay:** I know. We took a little while to get two moms together to get this on.

**Aimee:** Two busy moms. Yes, exactly. So, thank you so much for taking time out of your busy Saturday to come in and be here with me and share your story with us.

So, I wanted to start with your origin story. You've been running Nirvana for 19 years, which is an incredibly long time in the restaurant industry. Can you take us back to the beginning? What inspired you to open it? And what did those early days look like for you?

**Lindsay:** So, I started my culinary career early. My first job was at 15. I worked at Renaissance Cafe in Laguna Beach and I just loved it. I started as a barista and then I ended up doing desserts and then I ended up at Expo, and then that swung me into going to Northern California and living with my aunt and uncle. I worked in a coffee shop and then I went to New Mexico. I started Ayurvedic medicine. Then I went to Hawaii and my mom had an Ayurvedic retreat there and I worked in a little place called Postcards on the north shore of Kauai.

And then I came back and it was like the holidays and I just was fumbling, not sure exactly at 19 what to do with life. And I decided not to do Ayurvedic medicine like my mom, and I started culinary school. I was 19 and I went to Orange Coast College and it was just something that worked and everything I did I just was enthralled by and I wanted to do more.

So I started working for a bunch of caterers in Orange County and in LA - Fox Studios, Universal Studios, Sienna Catering, Pascal Hats, Sherman Gardens - and I just loved it. And so the more I did, the more I wanted to do more and I got great friendships. One of my great friends worked for Pascal in his restaurant and doing catering and he was short-handed. So it was an opening for me and so I started working for him. I worked for about a year, a year and a half.

And then I got a job while at Orange Coast at the Ritz Carlton. And I did both of them for about six months. So, I did pastry at the Ritz at 6:00 in the morning and then I'd get off, have a little break, and then go to Pascal's at night. And I did that for about six months concurrent. And then I ended up quitting to continue at the Ritz, which is where I met my at that time now ex-husband.

I just loved it and everywhere I could, I found areas that I could learn more. And so, I was running a program there. I was doing private chef work. And this was in like 2002 I think. And I ended up stopping at the Ritz after in 2004 I believe to start doing my private catering.

Then I got a job for Z Pizza as their corporate chef developing their whole program for all the franchises across the country because they'd sold like 200 franchise locations and we had to standardize everything, recipes and all of the purchasing and get a big purveyor that could do national distribution. And so that was a really interesting job because it took me in different directions. But then it became babysitting.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And the babysitting was wearing and taxing. I learned a lot outside of the realm that I already had learned. After that, Luis and I, my husband at the time, I was like, "What do you want to be doing?" Because I think I'm getting done here and I would really like to see about doing something independently, not just as a private chef, but maybe doing something else.

And we catered a lot and we did well. And so, I'm very big about manifesting. I know you know that. So, I got really clear about what that looked like for me. And so, it was February. We'd just been to the fancy food show in San Francisco and I came back and in March envisioned this whole thing and just was very intentional what I wanted it to look like, how big I wanted to be, how close to home I wanted it to be, what the food was going to be. We even developed a little menu for it.

And within a month, I got an email pop into my email that I had signed up for restaurantsforsale.com.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** Just to see anything in the area. I'd found it searching online and this one listing came up in Mission Viejo and I was like, "Well, that's interesting." All of them usually came in as a list.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** So, I went and checked it out and we ended up pulling money out of the house and buying it for like $100,000 and it was a turnkey restaurant ready to go and it was big enough and it had everything that I asked for.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** And so, we started there and then a year and a half went by and I'd gotten on the cover of a few magazines. Triple A had done a big feature on us in Westways and I was one of the top 10 chefs under 40 and I was 26 years old. Kind of a big deal and at the time I was one of the only female chefs too that were really in the market. Zov was there but there was not a lot of us.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And I got a lot of notoriety quickly.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** So, one day this cook came in the restaurant and he was looking for a job and he had worked in Laguna and his chef said to go see his friends that had a place in Mission Viejo because he went back to work after they were closed for a week and the restaurant was empty.

And so I'm in my office and my office was to the side and it was a big catering kitchen for the Mission Viejo restaurant and he's like, "Yeah, it was in Laguna." and I hear Laguna and I pop my desk chair on wheels out of the office and I'm like where was this? And so I call my broker and I'm like, "Dan, we need to go look at this place." And it was the restaurant that we're in right now for Laguna.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** And so when we went and looked, I found out that week that I was pregnant with Diego.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** And so we're trying to negotiate all this while just finding out I'm pregnant with my first son.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And I'm thinking, "Okay, well this is interesting. God really supports a lot because we had said we wanted a baby and then we had had this conversation of we're getting ready to grow again and they both happened simultaneously and I'm like well we can't say no to either of these things so we're just going to have to figure this problem out when we get there.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And so we just went for it.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And it was amazing and so we had both of them running concurrently for 5 years. Then when Mission Viejo closed it was when the whole economic downturn and Mission Viejo is such a bedroom community. It was not advantageous for us to continue after our lease and they had tripled our rent when they bought the building. So it was just not financially sustainable any longer.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And so now we've been in Laguna for 17 years. Diego just turned 17.

**Aimee:** Holy moly.

**Lindsay:** And so yeah, it's been quite the road.

**Aimee:** It's just amazing that it's been 17 years. Time flies when you're having fun. My gosh, it's amazing. I remember when you were pregnant with Diego and you craved goat cheese ice cream, so you ate it.

**Lindsay:** Well, the goat cheese - No, the goat cheese ice cream came in Mission Viejo the first year we were open.

**Aimee:** I guess I crave the goat cheese.

**Lindsay:** Danielle, my sister told that story and it was like she told her when she was a server to the guests that I'm like that didn't happen. She also told the story that I was doing something and goat cheese fell into the ice cream and I made it. I'm like, that didn't happen either.

**Aimee:** That's so funny. So, that's just an old Nirvana myth then.

**Lindsay:** That was my sister's way of selling it.

**Aimee:** I love it. It worked for me. It worked for me. The goat cheese ice cream is amazing and it's the only place I've ever tasted goat cheese ice cream. And you still have it on the menu, don't you?

**Lindsay:** It's a staple for 19 years. It was really just out of necessity. I had a fig tree and it was a tiny fig tree. I got it at Rogers Gardens.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And it was from Monrovia Plants and they had beautiful fruit trees and I planted it and it just had this bumper crop of figs and they were good-sized figs. And then as time went on, I mean I put it next to a phone base, the old cordless phone bases and it was the same size as a phone base. These were Turkish figs that were just gorgeous.

We were doing an appetizer and it was honeyed goat cheese with some black pepper and then we roasted them stuffed and then we'd wrap them in prosciutto and put them on a bed of arugula with some balsamic reduction. They were killer, right? And then we were doing a bananas foster as a dessert.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And so I thought, okay, we have all these figs. What am I going to do with all these figs? And so I thought, well, what if we did figs instead of the bananas? And what if we did Grand Marnier instead of the rum? What would that look like?

And so we did. And then the vanilla ice cream was "Okay, well, it's okay, but it's kind of flat." And then I was like, "Well, what about if we did something like whipped goat cheese into the vanilla custard instead?" And we tried that and it was so killer, right? But then the honey that we put to sweeten it was gritty. So I was like, "Okay, we got to change that."

So we did maple syrup instead and it just stuck.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** And so we've been doing it that way ever since.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** And so apricots are amazing on it. Strawberries are great on it. Pineapple's okay. I'm not a fan of it, but that's what we started with knowing that we could do things other than figs was a regular client saying, "Well, you don't have figs. Can you do another fruit and just do it the same way?"

**Aimee:** That sounds so - I could go for some right now, actually. I love it. And that's the creativity. I mean, literally, talk about farm to table. You're literally growing the figs in your backyard that are inspiring your food. That's pretty amazing. And you didn't see that as much back then, you know? I feel like the whole farm to table thing wasn't even around yet in 2004, '05, '06.

**Lindsay:** It's definitely been a - our family, that's how we were raised. Our family, my mother was one of the founding people with the co-op in the canyon. We got mountain people. We divided it between everybody. Everybody put their order down. And my mom and dad, that's how we lived.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And we grew up, they never owned a microwave. We always ate organically. And so now the fad is not a fad anymore. And we're not the weird kids that have the metal stainless steel boxes at school with steamed tofu and Bragg's and I don't even - We were the weird kids with food, but it was healthy food and we were raised that way. And so our palates are a little different.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** Right. And our whole family cooks and so we all have that way of being creative. And when we'd come home from school, it'd be "What are we going to eat?" And we all had to cook. If we wanted popcorn, it's not throw it in the microwave. We had to get the air popper out. We had to heat up the ghee. Then we had brewer's yeast and Spike.

**Aimee:** Yum.

**Lindsay:** And so now people are like, "Oh my god, that's amazing." But that was our everyday. And we're like, "Yeah, we don't have cool popcorn that's in the microwave." That was our thing that we didn't have. Or people are getting hot dogs and we're thinking, "Oh, we're so deprived." But now we look back and we're we're kind of glad because we were raised in a way that now what we eat has always been what's been good for us.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Absolutely. Yeah. You guys have a different relationship with food and food was always a creative outlet for you guys because cooking was just the way that you ate and it was always the time we got brought together.

**Lindsay:** Yes.

**Aimee:** Right. And I think that's what's always connected me with food is the memories that we created with them. Yeah, we're sitting around the table making spanakopita and we're all dipping it in butter and eating it right as we're folding them or we're shucking corn or we're doing peas that are English peas out of the shell. And those memories stuck with me.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** Because it was when we were all together.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And there was no conflict. We weren't proving anything. We were just cooking.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And nothing else seemed to be in our space.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Such a sacred space and time for families to be in when they're just in the kitchen together. I think of the special times when my mom comes, she always cooks with the kids. Every single one of them I have pictures of my mom in the kitchen with the kids and it's such a sweet time. And you're right, it does instill - now they want to cook, you know, because life goes on pause for a moment.

**Lindsay:** Yes, it does, even just for a moment. And during COVID we saw that a lot. We were providing food for people and they were cooking at home. We weren't cooking, we were giving them raw product and they were cooking at home and we were seeing people inspired by actually coming together again and spending time in the kitchen and what did that look like. And I think even as a restaurateur we have seen that trend keep that people do spend more time at home now.

They do cook more at home. The dining isn't as prolific as it used to be dining. Or they're more boxes and subscriptions people get to be able to make it easier and faster, but they're doing it at home, right? So, there's been this big changeover from that.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And that's - it's important that you make those changes. We call that adjust and conquer in our business. We did a big A&C during COVID also because we also saw big changes at that time. And it's, you know, if you can make the changes to suit your customers as their needs change, you can stay in business. But if you don't pivot, you know. So, it's nice that you were able to go ahead and do your rebrand to meet the people where they're at post, during and post-COVID. You did a big rebrand during that time.

**Lindsay:** Yeah. Life was very different with COVID on. Divorce and relationships and I started doing woodwork out of a relationship that I had and I just got to be creative and I got to have the freedom to design it the way I needed it, which I never felt the freedom to do in my business.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lindsay:** It's like COVID gave me the excuse to choose differently.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And I really embraced that part, right? Because I finally said, "Okay, this is time that I get to choose. This is the way I need to do it. This is the way my family needs to operate if I'm going to continue doing it."

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** At first, we were only open 4 days a week.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And that's what worked because I was trying to figure out life as a single mom.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And how did that look and how could I be there and how could I have child care all the time where a restaurant is not predictable?

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And so I had the kids on the days I was off.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And I didn't have to worry about child care on the days that I had them.

**Aimee:** And so I had to shift things around and we started doing events more and focusing towards that. Right now most of the focus that we have on a day-to-day is about our relationships with clients with events.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And they're long-standing. The Laguna Playhouse, we have a contract for the year. We're starting to work with UCI Health and UCI Mind and Rivian and the museum. The call that I was on right before we started.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** So we're doing things that are more predictable for income, more predictable for what we can give, more predictable for the community and what we can do as outreach and it gives us an ability to have security.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** Where the restaurant is just gravy.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** Right. We're there but it's gravy. It's not what makes us successful.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And so if it's not busy one day, it's not what we need to have happen. So, we've spent time and money and we're still at that infrastructure where we're not there yet, but we've spent that time this last year in our rebrand to build that directionality so we can be more sustainable for the future.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it's so fun. As we come into middle age, we come to a place of confidence where we can come to a place of reinvention of ourselves and our businesses. And I think we can - at that point there's a maturity level and a knowledge base in our fields that we can do it better than we did it in the first iteration you know and how hard do we want to work?

**Lindsay:** Exactly.

**Aimee:** Exactly.

**Lindsay:** Before I was the workhorse.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** I was the operator not the owner. Right. And now I've been able to hire staff that supports me and let go enough of what I thought was perfection was really control.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And the control didn't allow me the autonomy to do the other things that I needed.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** Because then I never had the time.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** And now it's like, oh, okay, here's a contract. You know what to do. Do it. And sometimes I come in and I'm on fire and I'm like, why have I been off and this is what it is. But I have a lot more confidence in them to figure it out.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** Or to teach them how to do it differently if it's not done right.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** Than before.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Lindsay:** It gives me the support that I need to do things outside of the restaurant.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. That's really important. I was just talking to Scout about this, how, you know, companies when we first start them, they're like newborn babies and they need us 24/7. But the idea is to raise our companies almost like we raise our children to be more independent to have systems and operating procedures that we can then step back and work on the business and not so much in it.

**Lindsay:** Business. Yeah. Absolutely. And as you get older, you're a little more tired.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I mean, honestly, menopause kicked my butt. I mean, yeah. I mean, I'm not fully in it yet, but we're a family of perimenopause very early. My aunt was 38, my mom was 42, I was 42. It just gets us early. And it is amazing the effect. I know you're not there yet, but the effect is just like, whoa, where did my memory go? Why am I not sleeping well? My weight, I didn't change anything. And what - how - what happened?

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. No, I mean this is a conversation that I was having with my girlfriends this week actually. We were talking about how women never really talk about menopause.

**Lindsay:** Oh, it's such a big thing and how really how much it really impacts your life as you're going through it and the fact that we're really not prepared for it. You know, it's - I think when you're a little kid, people prepare you to go through puberty.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** But you're not as prepared to go through what I like to call cougar puberty as you go through menopause. It's like you're not really as prepared for this, you know, nobody talks about it.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lindsay:** And it's such a thing because it's like your sex drive and everything - it's not a bad thing. It's actually I think in your 40s your sex drive increases.

**Aimee:** It does actually. It does.

**Lindsay:** Okay. Well, this is a great time of life, you know, especially being single, but it's like how do you deal with this? And then how do you have that conversation? And do you feel insecure about even relationships and going into that?

**Aimee:** Right.
